summary:
The island of Melos (Milos) in the central Cyclades of the Aegean Sea was the source of one of the most extensively traded materials in prehistoric Europe and the eastern Mediterranean — obsidian. Melian obsidian, a dark volcanic glass produced in the island's ancient lava flows, has been found at sites across the eastern Mediterranean from at least 10,000 BCE onward, demonstrating that seafarers were regularly crossing open water to obtain this highly prized material for stone tool production thousands of years before the Neolithic.

During the period 7000 to 6000 BCE, the distribution of Melian obsidian expanded alongside the establishment of farming communities throughout the Aegean and eastern Mediterranean. Obsidian, when skillfully knapped, produces a cutting edge sharper than surgical steel, making it invaluable for cutting tools before metal became available. The specific trace element composition of obsidian from different volcanic sources — including Melos, Lipari in the Tyrrhenian Sea, Anatolia, and other sources — allows archaeologists to identify the origin of obsidian artifacts through geochemical analysis (obsidian hydration and X-ray fluorescence studies), mapping ancient trade networks with remarkable precision.

The obsidian trade centered on Melos is significant as evidence for organized, long-distance maritime exchange in the prehistoric Aegean. Obtaining Melian obsidian required sea voyages of at least 150 kilometers in open water, demonstrating early Neolithic seafaring capability. This exchange also implies social relationships between island and mainland communities — perhaps kinship networks, reciprocal gift exchange, or more formalized trading relationships — that provided the framework within which valuable materials moved. The Melian obsidian trade represents one of the best-documented prehistoric exchange networks in the world.
